**John Frederick Milius** ([/ˈmɪliəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born April 11, 1944) is an American film director, screenwriter, and producer. He is considered a member of the [New Hollywood](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood") generation of filmmakers.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

He rose to prominence in the early 1970s for writing the scripts for *[The Life and Times of Judge Roy Bean](/wiki/The_Life_and_Times_of_Judge_Roy_Bean "The Life and Times of Judge Roy Bean")* (1972), [*Jeremiah Johnson*](/wiki/Jeremiah_Johnson_(film) "Jeremiah Johnson (film)") (also 1972), and the first two *[Dirty Harry](/wiki/Dirty_Harry_(film_series) "Dirty Harry (film series)")* films. He made his directorial debut with the film [*Dillinger*](/wiki/Dillinger_(1973_film) "Dillinger (1973 film)") (1973), followed by *[The Wind and the Lion](/wiki/The_Wind_and_the_Lion "The Wind and the Lion")* (1975) and *[Big Wednesday](/wiki/Big_Wednesday "Big Wednesday")* (1978). In 1980, he was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay") for *[Apocalypse Now](/wiki/Apocalypse_Now "Apocalypse Now")*, which he co-wrote with [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola").

During the 1980s, Milius established himself as a director of [action](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") and [adventure films](/wiki/Adventure_films "Adventure films"), with *[Conan the Barbarian](/wiki/Conan_the_Barbarian_(1982_film) "Conan the Barbarian (1982 film)")* (1982) and *[Red Dawn](/wiki/Red_Dawn "Red Dawn")* (1984). He was also a sought-after [script doctor](/wiki/Script_doctor "Script doctor"). He later served as the co-creator of the [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award")-winning television series [*Rome*](/wiki/Rome_(TV_series) "Rome (TV series)") (2005–2007).

Off-screen, Milius is known for his eccentric personality and libertarian political views, variously and contradictorily self-described as a "[Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen") [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism")", "right-wing extremist", and "[Maoist](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism")".[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) He served as a director of the [National Rifle Association of America](/wiki/National_Rifle_Association_of_America "National Rifle Association of America") (NRA).[[3]](#cite_note-Maverick2-3)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Milius was born April 11, 1944, in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri, the youngest of three children to Elizabeth Marie ([née](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Roe; 1906–2010) and William Styx Milius (1889–1975), who was a shoe manufacturer.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5) When Milius was seven, his father sold Milius Shoe Company, which his grandfather George W. Milius had founded in 1923,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and retired. He moved the family to [Bel Air](/wiki/Bel_Air,_Los_Angeles "Bel Air, Los Angeles"), California.[[7]](#cite_note-7) John Milius became an enthusiastic surfer. At 14, his parents sent him to a small private school, the [Lowell Whiteman School](/wiki/Steamboat_Mountain_School "Steamboat Mountain School"), in the mountains of [Steamboat Springs, Colorado](/wiki/Steamboat_Springs,_Colorado "Steamboat Springs, Colorado"), because he "was a juvenile delinquent".[[8]](#cite_note-cnn-8)

Milius became a voracious reader and started to write short stories: "I had learned very early, to write in almost any style. I could write in fluent [Hemingway](/wiki/Ernest_Hemingway "Ernest Hemingway"), or in fluent [Melville](/wiki/Herman_Melville "Herman Melville"), or [Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad"), or [Jack Kerouac](/wiki/Jack_Kerouac "Jack Kerouac"), and whatever."[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5) He says he was also influenced by the oral story telling of surfers at the time, who had a [beatnik](/wiki/Beatnik "Beatnik") tradition.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

"My religion is [surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing")", Milius said in 1976, adding that "the other thing that influenced me throughout my youth was my involvement with things Japanese. I studied [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo"), [kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo"), and painting. I felt more comfortable with things Japanese and with Japanese people than I did with Europeans ... feudalism in any country, at any period, fascinates me ... I understand the reasoning of people in Asia, it makes sense to me. Zen is very sensible, the whole way of feeling things is logical, whereas many of the Western-motivated things—greed, business sense—I'm not comfortable with, I don't understand their rationale."[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10)

Milius says he attempted to join the [Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") and volunteer for [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") service in the late 1960s, but was rejected due to a "chronic" and "sometimes disabling" case of mild [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma"). "I'd have given anything to be a Marine", said Milius. "As a surfer I'd spent a lot of time hanging out with the Marines off [Pendleton](/wiki/Marine_Corps_Base_Camp_Pendleton "Marine Corps Base Camp Pendleton"), and I'd had every intention of joining up ... I was devastated, I felt like I'd been rejected as a human being."[[11]](#cite_note-iraq-11) "It was totally demoralizing", he said later. "I missed going to my war. It probably caused me to be obsessed with war ever since."[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5) Milius said he was "dying to be able to ... go prove myself in battle—the same as all young men long to do, if they are honest with themselves, whether it's right or wrong or even sane, which is a debate that's been going on since we left the caves. Only there was no way I could found my own unit, so I did the second best, which was to write it. Every writer wishes he could actually be doing the thing he writes about."[[11]](#cite_note-iraq-11) He later admitted, "I don't know how well I'd have done. I really wanted a military career, to be a general, but I had a hard time polishing shoes. And marching. I was in the [ROTC](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") once, and I hate marching ... I would have been good in the Mexican Army."[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10)

At one stage Milius considered becoming an artist or historian. During a rainy day on a summer vacation in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") in 1962, he stumbled upon a movie theatre showing a week of [Akira Kurosawa](/wiki/Akira_Kurosawa "Akira Kurosawa") films and fell in love with cinema.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Milius studied film at the [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") [School of Cinema-Television](/wiki/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts "USC School of Cinematic Arts"), which he chose because it was an elitist school that trained people for Hollywood.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPyeMyles1979175-13) His classmates included [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas"), [Walter Murch](/wiki/Walter_Murch "Walter Murch"), [Basil Poledouris](/wiki/Basil_Poledouris "Basil Poledouris"), [Randal Kleiser](/wiki/Randal_Kleiser "Randal Kleiser") and [Donald F. Glut](/wiki/Donald_F._Glut "Donald F. Glut"). Milius says he was influenced by his teacher, Irwin Blacker:

> He gave you the screenplay form, which I hated so much, and if you made one mistake on the form, you flunked the class. His attitude was that the least you can learn is the form. "I can't grade you on the content. I can't tell you whether this is a better story for you to write than that, you know? And I can't teach you how to write the content, but I can certainly demand that you do it in the proper form." He never talked about character arcs or anything like that; he simply talked about telling a good yarn, telling a good story. He said, "Do whatever you need to do. Be as radical and as outrageous as you can be. Take any kind of approach you want to take. Feel free to flash back, feel free to flash forward, feel free to flash back in the middle of a flashback. Feel free to use narration, all the tools are there for you to use."[[14]](#cite_note-creative-14)

Milius says his writing style was influenced by two novels in particular, *[Moby-Dick](/wiki/Moby-Dick "Moby-Dick")* and *[On the Road](/wiki/On_the_Road "On the Road")*:

> I think *Moby Dick* is the best work of art ever made ... I used to point out the dramatic entrance of characters, how they were threaded through ... *Moby Dick* was a perfect screenplay, a perfect example of the kind of drama that I was interested in. Another great influence on me was ... *On the Road*, which has no tight, linear narrative, but sprawls, following this character. *Moby Dick* and *On the Road* are completely different kinds of novels, yet they're both extremely disciplined. Nothing happens by accident in either of those two books.[[14]](#cite_note-creative-14)

Milius reflected his "ambitions stopped at B Westerns ... I thought that was a good life. I never wanted to be [Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") or some big mogul, I didn't want to be [Louis B. Mayer](/wiki/Louis_B._Mayer "Louis B. Mayer"). I wanted to be ... [Budd Boetticher](/wiki/Budd_Boetticher "Budd Boetticher") or something ... [John Ford](/wiki/John_Ford "John Ford")."[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5)
His short films at film school included *The Reversal of Richard Sun* (1966), *Glut* (1967) and *Viking Women Don't Care* (1967). He wrote a documentary, *The Emperor* (1967), directed by classmate [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas"), who also edited an animated short Milius directed called *[Marcello, I'm So Bored](/wiki/Marcello,_I%27m_So_Bored "Marcello, I'm So Bored")* (1967) with John Strawbridge.

*Marcello*, Milius's thesis film, won best animation at the National Student Film Festival[[15]](#cite_note-15) and screened around the country in various festivals; it was praised by [Vincent Canby](/wiki/Vincent_Canby "Vincent Canby") of *The New York Times*.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Milius received a job offer to work in animation but he turned it down as he could not see himself "sitting there drawing cell after cell."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early")]

Milius's first completed script was *Los Gringos* (1968). "It actually wasn't bad", he later said. "It was sort of like *The Wild Bunch* ... there was a lot of killing and shooting and riding and dust ... sombreros. ... It was a pretty good idea, actually. It had everything, and it was certainly as original as *The Wild Bunch*, but it wasn't as skillfully written as later stuff."[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5)

He followed this with *The Last Resort* which was optioned by Michael S Laughlin in 1969.[[20]](#cite_note-Resort-20)[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5) Milius says, "Neither of them were ever made, but I was able to option them. I had them rented out for like $5,000 a year."[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21)

#### *The Devil's 8*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Devil's 8")]

Milius then got a summer job working at the story department of [American International Pictures](/wiki/American_International_Pictures "American International Pictures") through a student colleague of his who had begun working there, [Willard Huyck](/wiki/Willard_Huyck "Willard Huyck"). Huyck and Milius worked at AIP under producer [Larry Gordon](/wiki/Lawrence_Gordon_(producer) "Lawrence Gordon (producer)"), reading scripts. They eventually collaborated on a rewrite of the screenplay for *[The Devil's 8](/wiki/The_Devil%27s_8 "The Devil's 8")* (1968), an action drama about moonshine drivers which ripped off *[The Dirty Dozen](/wiki/The_Dirty_Dozen "The Dirty Dozen")* (1968).[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Milius's name had been mentioned in a 1968 *Time* magazine article about the new generation of Hollywood filmmakers, which also referred to [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas") and [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"). This was read by [Mike Medavoy](/wiki/Mike_Medavoy "Mike Medavoy"), who became Milius's agent. Medavoy called Milius "a badboy mad genius in a teenager's body, but he was a good and fast writer with original ideas."[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedavoy20136-23)

Milius began to get writing commissions. He wrote a script entitled *The Texans* for [Al Ruddy](/wiki/Albert_S._Ruddy "Albert S. Ruddy") at Paramount, a contemporary version of *[Red River](/wiki/Red_River_(1948_film) "Red River (1948 film)")* (1948)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMedavoy2013173-24) (never made, although [Sam Peckinpah](/wiki/Sam_Peckinpah "Sam Peckinpah") was going to direct it in 1979[[25]](#cite_note-25))— Milius later said it "wasn't very good".[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21) He also wrote an original called *Truck Driver* (aka *The Haul*) which was purchased by Levy-Garner-Laven,[[26]](#cite_note-Haul-26) although that film too was not made.

Milius later said he "didn't do a good job" with these two early scripts "because in both cases I was influenced by the people who had hired me. They said put this in and put that in, and I went along with it. Every time I went along with something in my whole career it usually didn't work. Usually there's a price to pay. You think of selling out, but there is a price to pay. Usually what people want you to do is make it current."[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21)

#### *Jeremiah Johnson*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Jeremiah Johnson")]

Milius then wrote *[Jeremiah Johnson](/wiki/Jeremiah_Johnson_(film) "Jeremiah Johnson (film)")*, a story loosely based on the life of the [mountain man](/wiki/Mountain_man "Mountain man") [Liver-Eating Johnson](/wiki/Liver-Eating_Johnson "Liver-Eating Johnson").

Milius later said this was "the real breaking point" where he knew "almost overnight ... that I had become a good writer with a voice.":

> I knew that material. I'd lived in the mountains, I had a trapline, I hunted, and I had a lot of experiences with characters up there. So, it was real easy to write that and there was a humor to it, a kind of bigger-than-life attitude. I was inspired by [Carl Sandburg](/wiki/Carl_Sandburg "Carl Sandburg"). I read a lot of his poetry and it's this kind of abrupt description—"a train is coming, thundering steel, where are you going? Wichita." That great kind of feeling that he had, that's what I was trying to do there. I remember there was a great poem about American braggarts. You know, American liars—"I am the ring-tailed cousin to the such and such that ate so and so and I can do this and I can do that better than [Mike Fink](/wiki/Mike_Fink "Mike Fink") the river man ..." I just realized that this was the voice that the script had to have. It was as clear as a bell. I knew that writing was particular to me.[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21)

Milius sold the script to [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") in 1970 for $5,000, going up to $50,000 if it was ever made. Warner Bros. had other writers work on the original script based on *The Crow Killer*. Milius was also called back to work on it, and his fee grew each time. (He eventually made $90,000 on the film.[[27]](#cite_note-auto-27)) Eventually, [Robert Redford](/wiki/Robert_Redford "Robert Redford") agreed to play the lead and [Sydney Pollack](/wiki/Sydney_Pollack "Sydney Pollack") signed to direct.

#### *Dirty Harry* and *Judge Roy Bean*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Dirty Harry and Judge Roy Bean")]

Milius wrote an uncredited draft of *[Dirty Harry](/wiki/Dirty_Harry "Dirty Harry")* (1971). He says his contribution to the film was "A lot of guns. And the attitude of Dirty Harry, being a cop who was ruthless. I think it's fairly obvious if you look at the rest of my work what parts are mine. The cop being the same as the killer except he has a badge. And being lonely."[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10) *Dirty Harry* was an enormous box office hit.

George Hamilton hired Milius to rewrite *[Evel Knievel](/wiki/Evel_Knievel_(1971_film) "Evel Knievel (1971 film)")* (1971), a biopic of the [stunt rider](/wiki/Evel_Knievel "Evel Knievel"), at a fee of $1,000 a day. Milius re-did the entire script over seven days.[[27]](#cite_note-auto-27)

He wrote an original script, *[The Life and Times of Judge Roy Bean](/wiki/The_Life_and_Times_of_Judge_Roy_Bean "The Life and Times of Judge Roy Bean")*, about the [famous judge](/wiki/Roy_Bean "Roy Bean"). He offered it for $150,000 if he could direct, but could find no takers. He sold it to [First Artists](/wiki/First_Artists "First Artists") for $300,000, then extremely high for a script.[[14]](#cite_note-creative-14) Directed by [John Huston](/wiki/John_Huston "John Huston") and starring [Paul Newman](/wiki/Paul_Newman "Paul Newman"), it was a moderate hit, although Milius disliked the final result. "I fought every day", he said. "And I was blooded well. I was treated horribly."[[28]](#cite_note-28) More popular was *Jeremiah Johnson*.

Milius did some work with [David Giler](/wiki/David_Giler "David Giler") on the script which became *[The Black Bird](/wiki/The_Black_Bird "The Black Bird")*.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

By now Milius was one of the most sought after screenwriters in Hollywood, seen as a colorful character with a talent for lively interviews. His self-styled "Zen Anarchist"/"American samurai" persona made him stand out in Hollywood.[[30]](#cite_note-30) For instance, he rewrote *Dirty Harry* only on the condition that he be given an expensive gun.[[31]](#cite_note-31) He was also one of the inspirations for the character of John Milner in the enormously successful *[American Graffiti](/wiki/American_Graffiti "American Graffiti")* (1973).[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10) Milius said of this film, "I guess he [Lucas] saw me in that light because I was a surfer going past my time."[[32]](#cite_note-32)

He also wrote the first draft of the *Dirty Harry* sequel, *[Magnum Force](/wiki/Magnum_Force "Magnum Force")* (1973).[[33]](#cite_note-33) Milius later said "I don't like *Magnum Force*. Of all the films I had anything to do with, I like it least. They changed a lot of things in a cheap and distasteful manner."[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10) It was however successful at the box office.

#### *Dillinger*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Dillinger")]

Milius wanted to move behind the camera. "Being a director is the only way anyone will listen to you in Hollywood", he said. "It's the next best thing to being a star."[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Gangster films were popular at the time and [AIP](/wiki/American_International_Pictures "American International Pictures") offered him the chance to direct one if he would write it for a fraction of his regular fee.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5) Milius agreed and wrote and directed *[Dillinger](/wiki/Dillinger_(1973_film) "Dillinger (1973 film)")* (1973). "I deliberately chose Dillinger because he was a pure criminal", said Milius. "Robbing banks to right social wrongs did not come into it."[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The movie was moderately successful and launched Milius's directing career. He worked on the script for a TV sequel, *[Melvin Purvis: G-Man](/wiki/Melvin_Purvis:_G-Man "Melvin Purvis: G-Man")* (1974), a pilot for a proposed series about [Melvin Purvis](/wiki/Melvin_Purvis "Melvin Purvis") (there was a second TV movie, but no series), but did not like the director, [Dan Curtis](/wiki/Dan_Curtis "Dan Curtis"), or the experience of working for TV.[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10)

Contemporary film critics grouped Milius in with the emerging "movie brats" generation of filmmakers that also including Lucas, Coppola, [Terrence Malick](/wiki/Terrence_Malick "Terrence Malick"), and Scorsese.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In 1974, [David Picker](/wiki/David_Picker "David Picker") announced he would produce *Ranch Life and the Hunting Trail* directed by Milius and written by [Winfred Blevins](/wiki/Winfred_Blevins "Winfred Blevins"), about [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"). The film was never made.[[38]](#cite_note-Ranch-38) Neither was *The Life and Times of Joe McCarthy*, a proposed biopic about the [famous anti-Communist Senator](/wiki/Joseph_McCarthy "Joseph McCarthy"), which Milius declared interest in making.[[39]](#cite_note-McCarthy-39)

#### *The Wind and the Lion*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Wind and the Lion")]

Milius next wrote and directed the popular adventure film *[The Wind and the Lion](/wiki/The_Wind_and_the_Lion "The Wind and the Lion")* (1975), which starred [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery") and [Candice Bergen](/wiki/Candice_Bergen "Candice Bergen"). He later said he felt this was his first "real" movie.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

He intended to follow this with *Give Your Heart to the Hawks*, a story about mountain man [Jedediah Smith](/wiki/Jedediah_Smith "Jedediah Smith") in the 1820s based on the novel by Winfred Blevins[[42]](#cite_note-hawks-42) "It's my interpretation of Jedediah Smith, which might not be exactly historical", said Milius. "It'll be about exploration, about the need to see what's over the next ridge and what that does, what price you pay, to find out. Like Dirty Harry, Smith is a classic lone man, with a searing loneliness about him. A leader of men is always alone."[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10) It was never made; neither was *Man-Eaters of Kumoan* (1976) based on book by [Jim Corbett](/wiki/Jim_Corbett "Jim Corbett") about a tiger hunter in India which Milius worked on.[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10)

For several years, he developed with [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") an adaptation of *Night Drop* by [S. L. A. Marshall](/wiki/S._L._A._Marshall "S. L. A. Marshall"). "We talked about it for years and years and worked on it for a long time, but we never did it."[[43]](#cite_note-CS-43)

Milius did come close to making *[Extreme Prejudice](/wiki/Extreme_Prejudice_(film) "Extreme Prejudice (film)")*, based on his script, in 1976. However he decided to make *Big Wednesday* instead; *Extreme Prejudice* would be made a decade later, much rewritten, and directed by [Walter Hill](/wiki/Walter_Hill "Walter Hill").

#### *Big Wednesday* and the A Team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Big Wednesday and the A Team")]

In 1975, Milius formed his own production company, The A Team, with [Buzz Feitshans](/wiki/Buzz_Feitshans "Buzz Feitshans"), who had edited *Dillinger*. They had a five-year deal with Warner Bros.. Milius said, "Our motto is Civitas Sine Prudentia, which really translates to Social Irresponsibility; I believe in it. It's refreshing, it's liberating. Americans are basically socially irresponsible ... Who else would have invented the atomic bomb quite the same way? The Nazis would have invented it with the desire to conquer the world; we were the only people that could have invented it with the desire not to conquer the world"[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10)

Its first production was an autobiographical surfing picture, *[Big Wednesday](/wiki/Big_Wednesday "Big Wednesday")* (1978),[[44]](#cite_note-44) which he called "a surfing *[How Green Was My Valley](/wiki/How_Green_Was_My_Valley "How Green Was My Valley")*".[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10) This was a major commercial disappointment although it has gone on to be a cult film.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Milius's friendship with George Lucas saw him given a percentage of the profits for *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")*, which Mike Medavoy estimated earned Milius $1.5 million—in exchange Milius gave Lucas a percentage of the profits for *Big Wednesday* which amounted to virtually nothing.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In 1979, Milius said "the ultimate aim of the A Team is that it will become a company that makes lots of projects. I shall be the figurehead and the father figure and take a percentage and I won't have to do anything except go off and direct my movie once every three years."[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The A Team made a number of movies not directed by Milius. Notably, they produced the first three films from [Robert Zemeckis](/wiki/Robert_Zemeckis "Robert Zemeckis") and [Bob Gale](/wiki/Bob_Gale "Bob Gale"): *[I Wanna Hold Your Hand](/wiki/I_Wanna_Hold_Your_Hand_(film) "I Wanna Hold Your Hand (film)")*, *[1941](/wiki/1941_(film) "1941 (film)")* (directed by [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")), and *[Used Cars](/wiki/Used_Cars "Used Cars")*.[[48]](#cite_note-48) He also produced *Hardcore*, directed by friend [Paul Schrader](/wiki/Paul_Schrader "Paul Schrader").

Schrader once described Milius's writing as containing too many good lines and scenes. He says [Warren Beatty](/wiki/Warren_Beatty "Warren Beatty") once "told John something I've been telling him too: 'You come too soon and you come too often.'... He's so full of juice he just can't stop coming, rather than holding back and tightening the situation and building characters. That releasing diffuses the energy, the characters are too broad because they never have time to build up the inner strength."[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### *Apocalypse Now*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Apocalypse Now")]

Milius says he was offered $17,000 to rewrite *[Skin Game](/wiki/Skin_Game "Skin Game")* (1971) but then [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola") made a competing offer of $15,000 for Milius to write *Apocalypse Now*.[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5) *Apocalypse Now* was an adaptation of *[Heart of Darkness](/wiki/Heart_of_Darkness "Heart of Darkness")* set in the Vietnam War[[50]](#cite_note-50) which George Lucas intended to direct as a follow-up to his first feature *[THX 1138](/wiki/THX_1138 "THX 1138")* (1971).[[51]](#cite_note-51) Milius says Coppola:

> Offered that wonderful fork in the road where I could go do my own thing rather than just rewrite some piece of crap that would probably be rewritten by somebody else. That was the most important decision I made in my life as a writer. That sort of steered me onto the path of doing my own work and being a little more like a novelist ... I tackled an unpopular subject that no one was going to make a movie about where the chances were really slim that I could pull it off. There was no book, nothing but me and the blank page. And that was wonderful because I had followed my heart. One of the nicest times in my life was writing *Apocalypse Now.*[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21)

The commercial failure of *THX 1138* delayed production plans for *Apocalypse Now*. Milius later said of the *Apocalypse Now* script, "No one would touch it because of the Vietnam War. Everyone loved it, it did more for my career than any other script because it was always considered a work of genius; from the minute it came out, it really stirred people up. It's a good script, it's certainly no work of genius. It churns people up, and that's what they think works of genius are supposed to do."[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10)

However, the following year saw the release of *[Apocalypse Now](/wiki/Apocalypse_Now "Apocalypse Now")*, directed by Francis Ford Coppola. Coppola rewrote the script, which Milius disliked. "He wanted to ruin it, liberalize it, and turn it into *[Hair](/wiki/Hair_(musical) "Hair (musical)")*", said Milius in 1976. "He sees himself as a great humanitarian, an enlightened soul who will tell you such wonderful things as he does at the end of *[Godfather 2](/wiki/Godfather_2 "Godfather 2")* -- that crime doesn't pay ... Talent-wise, he's no John Ford; character-wise, he's no Steve Spielberg. Francis can't stand to have any other creative influence around ... Francis Coppola has this compelling desire to save humanity when the man is a raving fascist, the [Bay Area](/wiki/Bay_Area "Bay Area") [Mussolini](/wiki/Mussolini "Mussolini")."[[10]](#cite_note-comment-10)

The film was released in 1979 to great acclaim.

Milius's old agent, Mike Medavoy, helped establish [Orion Pictures](/wiki/Orion_Pictures "Orion Pictures") in 1978 and one of their first movies was going to be *East of Suez*, written and directed by Milius.[[52]](#cite_note-Suez-52) It was not made.

Spielberg said in 1978 that Milius was key to the group of young filmmakers known as the [New Hollywood](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood"), which included himself, Lucas, and Coppola:

> John is our Scoutmaster. He's the one who will tell you to go on a trip and only take enough food, enough water for one day, and make you stay out longer than that. He's the one who says, "Be a man. I don't want to see any tears." He's a terrific raconteur, a wonderful story teller. John has more life than all the rest of us put together.[[53]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-53)

([Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino") said he could imagine the film *[Deliverance](/wiki/Deliverance "Deliverance")* being about "Hollywood filmmakers: you can imagine Spielberg, Lucas, and Scorsese as the husbands. And you can really imagine John Milius as Lewis."[[54]](#cite_note-54))

#### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1980s")]

In 1982, Milius directorial effort *[Conan the Barbarian](/wiki/Conan_the_Barbarian_(1982_film) "Conan the Barbarian (1982 film)")* was released, based on the fantasy novel by the same name. The film is credited for turning its lead, [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger"), into a star.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) Milius was interested in the project since the late 1970s. While he was about to get hired for scripting duties, he had to pass due to his commitment to *Big Wednesday*. Instead, [Oliver Stone](/wiki/Oliver_Stone "Oliver Stone") was hired to write the project. Eventually, producer [Dino De Laurentiis](/wiki/Dino_De_Laurentiis "Dino De Laurentiis") with whom Milius had contractual obligations joined the project. Milius returned to the project as its director re-working Stone's script. Upon its release the film was popular.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The film was the 15th highest grossing film in the USA that year, making $39,565,475 domestically.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In 1983, he was among the producers of [Ted Kotcheff](/wiki/Ted_Kotcheff "Ted Kotcheff")'s *[Uncommon Valor](/wiki/Uncommon_Valor "Uncommon Valor"),*[[59]](#cite_note-59) and credited as a "spiritual adviser" in the action film *[Lone Wolf McQuade](/wiki/Lone_Wolf_McQuade "Lone Wolf McQuade")*.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In 1984, he directed the popular action film, *[Red Dawn](/wiki/Red_Dawn "Red Dawn").*[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) The film is about a Russian invasion in the United States, and a rogue commando group of teenagers standing up to the invaders. Some critics perceived it to be "warmongering propaganda" while Milius said it was "anti-war".[[63]](#cite_note-63) The film was the 19th highest grossing film in the USA that year, making $38,376,497 domestically.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

He wrote and directed an episode for *[The New Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1985_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1985 TV series)")* (1985) and a story of his, "Viking Bikers from Hell", was used in an episode of *[Miami Vice](/wiki/Miami_Vice "Miami Vice")* ([season 3](/wiki/List_of_Miami_Vice_episodes#Season_3_(1986–87) "List of Miami Vice episodes"), episode 22).

In 1986, it was reported that he was writing the script for *[Fatal Beauty](/wiki/Fatal_Beauty "Fatal Beauty")* which he hoped to direct with [Cher](/wiki/Cher "Cher");[[65]](#cite_note-fatal-65) the film was made by [Tom Holland](/wiki/Tom_Holland_(filmmaker) "Tom Holland (filmmaker)") and starred [Whoopi Goldberg](/wiki/Whoopi_Goldberg "Whoopi Goldberg").

There was some talk that he would direct a movie for [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO"), *Capone*, but it was not made.[[66]](#cite_note-Capone-66)

In the late 1980s Milius wrote and directed a [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War") adventure film *[Farewell to the King](/wiki/Farewell_to_the_King "Farewell to the King")* (1989).[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) This flopped at the box office. In 1989, he tried to get funding for adaptations of [Allan W. Eckert](/wiki/Allan_W._Eckert "Allan W. Eckert")'s "The Frontiersmen: A Narrative", about settling the [Ohio River Valley](/wiki/Ohio_Country "Ohio Country"), and "Half of the Sky", about a [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") explorer.[[70]](#cite_note-:89-70)

[Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery") was hired to star in the film *[The Hunt for Red October](/wiki/The_Hunt_for_Red_October_(film) "The Hunt for Red October (film)")* for producer [Mace Neufeld](/wiki/Mace_Neufeld "Mace Neufeld"), based on [Tom Clancy](/wiki/Tom_Clancy "Tom Clancy")'s [novel of the same name](/wiki/The_Hunt_for_Red_October "The Hunt for Red October"). Connery thought the script was "too American" and insisted Neufeld hire John Milius to rewrite the Russian sequences. Connery thought with Milius, he could "get a different sort of image, different speech patterns."[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Neufeld then hired Milius to write and direct *[Flight of the Intruder](/wiki/Flight_of_the_Intruder "Flight of the Intruder")*, based on the book by [Stephen Coonts](/wiki/Stephen_Coonts "Stephen Coonts"). It too was not a financial success.

"I think the culture had changed and that is why my films were less accepted", he reflected later. "I still think those are also great films, *Farewell to the King* especially."[[72]](#cite_note-crave-72)

### Later

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Later")]

#### 1990s: screenwriting, cable TV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1990s: screenwriting, cable TV")]

In 1992, Milius claimed that he was blacklisted for his conservative beliefs in liberal Hollywood, saying that his flops were not as forgiven as those from more leftist directors. "It weighs ten times heavier against me", he said. "If you don't share the politically correct vision, then you are an outlaw, you are hunted and there is a price on your head, and if they catch you they will hang you."[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The film of *Hunt for the Red October* had been a big success, however, and Milius remained in high demand as a screenwriter: he did several drafts of another Clancy adaptation, *[Clear and Present Danger](/wiki/Clear_and_Present_Danger_(film) "Clear and Present Danger (film)")* (1994), which was another hit.

Milius worked on a number on unfilmed scripts, including *Bad Iron*, a biker movie written by Kent Anderson, which he intended to produce.[[74]](#cite_note-Bad-74) He was going to direct a film about [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") starring [Jean-Claude Van Damme](/wiki/Jean-Claude_Van_Damme "Jean-Claude Van Damme") but that was put on hold when a [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries") on the same topic was made by Italian TV.[[75]](#cite_note-Alex1-75)[[76]](#cite_note-Alex2-76) He wrote *[Harlot's Ghost](/wiki/Harlot%27s_Ghost "Harlot's Ghost")*, for Francis Ford Coppola, based on a novel by [Norman Mailer](/wiki/Norman_Mailer "Norman Mailer"); Milius described it as "a cross between *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")* and *Apocalypse Now.* It's about families and duplicity and danger, but this time provoked by the government."[[77]](#cite_note-Ghost-77)

He adapted the [Sgt. Rock](/wiki/Sgt._Rock "Sgt. Rock") comics for producer [Joel Silver](/wiki/Joel_Silver "Joel Silver"), with either [Renny Harlin](/wiki/Renny_Harlin "Renny Harlin") and [Paul Verhoeven](/wiki/Paul_Verhoeven "Paul Verhoeven") attached at certain points respectively. And also wrote a version of *Die Hard 3*, co-written with Barry Beckerman.[[78]](#cite_note-Hard-78) In the early 1990s he wrote *Texas Rangers*, about the establishment of that organization, for [Frank Price](/wiki/Frank_Price "Frank Price") at Columbia. He hoped to direct the film, but could not raise the funding.[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21)

In 1993, he replaced [Andrei Konchalovsky](/wiki/Andrei_Konchalovsky "Andrei Konchalovsky") as director on *The Northmen* for [Morgan Creek Productions](/wiki/Morgan_Creek_Entertainment_Group "Morgan Creek Entertainment Group"), about an English monk who gets captured by a band of Vikings. "This was inevitable", Milius said of his directing a Viking film. "I've been a practicing pagan for a long time. *Conan the Barbarian* was really a Viking movie but it was disguised."[[79]](#cite_note-Northmen2-79) However, financing fell through. He was going to direct an adaptation of [Tom Clancy](/wiki/Tom_Clancy "Tom Clancy")'s novel *[Without Remorse](/wiki/Without_Remorse "Without Remorse")* with [Gary Sinise](/wiki/Gary_Sinise "Gary Sinise") and [Laurence Fishburne](/wiki/Laurence_Fishburne "Laurence Fishburne"), but the project folded in 1995, two weeks before shooting was to commence due to the financial collapse of [Savoy Pictures](/wiki/Savoy_Pictures "Savoy Pictures").[[80]](#cite_note-Clancy2-80)

A Milius script that was filmed was his biopic of [Geronimo](/wiki/Geronimo "Geronimo"), *[Geronimo: An American Legend](/wiki/Geronimo:_An_American_Legend "Geronimo: An American Legend")*, for Walter Hill.

He also directed two films for cable: *Motorcycle Gang* (1994) and *[Rough Riders](/wiki/Rough_Riders_(miniseries) "Rough Riders (miniseries)")* (1997).

#### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In 2000, Milius was hired to work as a creative consultant with the [Institute for Creative Technologies](/wiki/Institute_for_Creative_Technologies "Institute for Creative Technologies") to pre-visualize the challenges to peace that America will face and the advanced virtual reality technologies necessary to train U.S. troops for the future. "Through his enormous body of work, John has shown a deep understanding of the human condition and the ways that conflict can be resolved", said ICT executive director Richard Lindheim. "Furthermore our efforts will benefit greatly from his vision of the world in the near future, and the techniques and procedures that will be needed to maintain security."[[81]](#cite_note-81)

That year he also wrote two biopics: *Le May* for Robert Zemeckis, about [Curtis LeMay](/wiki/Curtis_LeMay "Curtis LeMay");[[82]](#cite_note-Maverick-82) and *Manila John*, about [John Basilone](/wiki/John_Basilone "John Basilone"), which he was going to make for HBO. Warner Bros. wanted him to update *[Dirty Harry](/wiki/Dirty_Harry_(film_series) "Dirty Harry (film series)")* and he wanted them to fund a version of *[The Iliad](/wiki/The_Iliad "The Iliad")*; there was also talk he would make *The Alamo* for HBO.[[43]](#cite_note-CS-43)

In the early 2000s he worked on *King Conan: Crown of Iron* (2001−02), a sequel to *Conan the Barbarian*.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) Later, in 2019 Arnold Schwarzenegger met with Milius to discuss the status of the project. "We're on the same page," he said. "We both want to get it done."[[85]](#cite_note-85)

He also developed *Jornada del Muerto* (*Journey of Death*) (2003), a biker film starring [Triple H](/wiki/Triple_H "Triple H")[[86]](#cite_note-Jornada-86) and wrote a pilot for a TV show for UPN, *Delta*, about a military special ops team that takes on terrorists.[[87]](#cite_note-Delta-87) None of these movies were made.[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21)

*[Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(film) "Texas Rangers (film)")* (2001) was eventually made, though Milius stated that his script was substantially rewritten.

#### Financial difficulties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Financial difficulties")]

Milius suffered a major financial reversal in the late 1990s and early 2000s when his accountant embezzled from him an estimated $3 million.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

He tried to get a job as a staff writer on the TV show *[Deadwood](/wiki/Deadwood_(TV_series) "Deadwood (TV series)")*; showrunner [David Milch](/wiki/David_Milch "David Milch") was reluctant as he did not consider Milius a staff writer. Milius pleaded that he needed the money in order to pay for his son's tuition at law school, so Milch simply paid the fees. Milius's career recovered when he helped create the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")/HBO television series *[Rome](/wiki/Rome_(TV_series) "Rome (TV series)")*, which allowed him to repay Milch.

He wrote some pilots which did not go to series—*Dodge City* (circa 2005)—a Western series for CBS,[[89]](#cite_note-Dodge-89) and *Saigon Bureau* (2008)—about the AIP Bureau of [photojournalists](/wiki/Photojournalism "Photojournalism") in the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), a collaboration with [Chris Noth](/wiki/Chris_Noth "Chris Noth") based on the book *Requiem*.[[90]](#cite_note-Saigon-90) He also wrote a script about the [Battle of Chosin Reservoir](/wiki/Battle_of_Chosin_Reservoir "Battle of Chosin Reservoir") in the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), *The Chosin Few* for Mark Cuban's 2929 Entertainment,[[91]](#cite_note-Chosin-91) and *The Iron Horsemen*, a motorcycle feature.[[92]](#cite_note-Horsemen-92)

#### Health problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Health problems")]

In 2010 Milius was working on a new project, a film biography of [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan"),[[8]](#cite_note-cnn-8) and a proposed TV series called *Pharaoh*, set during the reign of Queen [Hatshepsut](/wiki/Hatshepsut "Hatshepsut"),[[93]](#cite_note-Pharaoh-93) when he had a stroke. For a while he was unable to speak or move, but ultimately he recovered.

#### Video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Video games")]

In March 2011, Milius was a story consultant for the video game *[Homefront](/wiki/Homefront_(video_game) "Homefront (video game)")*[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) about a North Korean conquest of America.

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Influence")]

Milius has long claimed to be an outsider in Hollywood. In 2001 he stated:

> I've always been considered a nut. They kind of tolerate me. It's certainly affected me. I've been blacklisted for a large part of my career because of my politics—as surely as any writer was blacklisted back in the 1950s. It's just that my politics are from the other side, and Hollywood always veers left.[[97]](#cite_note-guardian-97)

He wrote a number of iconic film lines such as "Charlie don't surf" and "I love the smell of napalm in the morning", from *Apocalypse Now*, and the famous [Harry Callahan](/wiki/Harry_Callahan_(character) "Harry Callahan (character)") one-liners delivered by [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood"), including "[Go ahead, make my day](/wiki/Go_ahead,_make_my_day "Go ahead, make my day")" and "Ask yourself one question, 'Do I feel lucky?' Well, do you, punk?" Milius also had a hand in the [USS *Indianapolis*](/wiki/USS_Indianapolis_(CA-35) "USS Indianapolis (CA-35)") monologue in the film *[Jaws](/wiki/Jaws_(film) "Jaws (film)")*;[[53]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-53) the sequence was performed by [Robert Shaw](/wiki/Robert_Shaw_(actor) "Robert Shaw (actor)"). When Spielberg asked him to contribute to the screenplay for *[Saving Private Ryan](/wiki/Saving_Private_Ryan "Saving Private Ryan")*, Milius suggested the opening and closing scenes at [Normandy cemetery](/wiki/Normandy_American_Cemetery_and_Memorial "Normandy American Cemetery and Memorial") where Ryan, now an elderly hero of World War II, in a moment of [survivor guilt](/wiki/Survivor_guilt "Survivor guilt"), asks his wife "Did I live a good life?"[[97]](#cite_note-guardian-97)

After his work on *Rough Riders* (1997), Milius became an instrumental force in lobbying Congress to award President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") the [Medal of Honor](/wiki/Medal_of_Honor "Medal of Honor") (posthumously), for acts of conspicuous gallantry while in the [Battle of San Juan Hill](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Juan_Hill "Battle of San Juan Hill").[[5]](#cite_note-IGN-5) Milius made two films featuring Roosevelt: *[The Wind and the Lion](/wiki/The_Wind_and_the_Lion "The Wind and the Lion")* (where he was played by [Brian Keith](/wiki/Brian_Keith "Brian Keith")) and the made-for-TV film *Rough Riders* (where [Tom Berenger](/wiki/Tom_Berenger "Tom Berenger") took the role).

The character of John Milner from the 1973 George Lucas film *[American Graffiti](/wiki/American_Graffiti "American Graffiti")* was inspired in part by Milius, who was a good friend of Lucas while they were at USC film school. Likewise, the character Walter Sobchak in the 1998 film *[The Big Lebowski](/wiki/The_Big_Lebowski "The Big Lebowski")* (portrayed by [John Goodman](/wiki/John_Goodman "John Goodman")) was partly inspired by Milius, a friend of [The Coen Brothers](/wiki/The_Coen_Brothers "The Coen Brothers"). The novella *Blind Jozef Pronek and Dead Souls* by [Aleksandar Hemon](/wiki/Aleksandar_Hemon "Aleksandar Hemon") features an episode with Milius, who is described as "sitting at a desk sucking on a cigar as long as a walking stick".

Milius was also instrumental during the startup of the UFC ([Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship")) organization: it was his idea to use the octagon-shaped cage, and his association with UFC helped provide interest and investors to the startup UFC.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

In 2013, a documentary about his life and career, titled *[Milius](/wiki/Milius_(film) "Milius (film)")*, was released.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

Writer Nat Segaloff called Milius:

> The best writer of the so-called USC Mafia, a tight-knit group that resuscitated—some say homogenised American cinema in the 1970s ... Raised on Ford, Hawks, Lean and Kurosawa, shaped by filmmakers as disparate as [Fellini](/wiki/Fellini "Fellini") and [Delmer Daves](/wiki/Delmer_Daves "Delmer Daves"), Milius favours history books over comic books, character over special effects, and heroes with roots in reality, time, place and customs. Milius' stories reflect his own deeply held ethic, which embraces the values of tradition, adventure, spiritualism, honour and an intense loyalty to friends ... Although he privately chafes at his public image as a gun-toting, liberal baiting provocateur, he allows himself to be painted as such, at times even holding the brush. He plays the Hollywood game like a pro, yet sticks to his own rules; he is a romantic filmmaker who avoids love scenes; his movies contain violence, yet no death in them is without meaning.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Milius himself once said:

> Never compromise excellence. To write for someone else is the biggest mistake that any writer makes. You should be your biggest competitor, your biggest critic, your biggest fan, because you don't know what anybody else thinks. How arrogant it is to assume that you know the market, that you know what's popular today—only Steven Spielberg knows what's popular today. Only Steven Spielberg will ever know what's popular. So leave it to him. He's the only one in the history of man who has ever figured that out. Write what you want to see. Because if you don't, you're not going to have any true passion in it, and it's not going to be done with any true artistry.[[21]](#cite_note-diary-21)

[Triple H](/wiki/Triple_H "Triple H"), retired [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE") wrestler and current Head of Creative for WWE, cites Milius as an inspiration in how to tell a compelling story within a wrestling match without having to rely on over-the-top action, as he stated that Milius did not rely on action in his work to tell a compelling story to the audience.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A character in the [2023 film](/wiki/2023_in_film "2023 in film") *[Rebel Moon](/wiki/Rebel_Moon "Rebel Moon")* is named after Milius as an homage from the film's co-writers [Kurt Johnstad](/wiki/Kurt_Johnstad "Kurt Johnstad") and [Zack Snyder](/wiki/Zack_Snyder "Zack Snyder").[[102]](#cite_note-102)

## Awards and honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Awards and honors")]

For writing the *Apocalypse Now* screenplay, Milius and Francis Ford Coppola were nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay"), and the [Writers Guild of America Award](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America_Award "Writers Guild of America Award") for [Best Drama Written Directly for the Screen](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America_Award_for_Best_Original_Screenplay "Writers Guild of America Award for Best Original Screenplay") (Though the film was an adaptation of *[Heart of Darkness](/wiki/Heart_of_Darkness "Heart of Darkness")*, the [Writers Guild](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America "Writers Guild of America") considered it an original screenplay.).

In 2007, Milius was the recipient of the [Austin Film Festival](/wiki/Austin_Film_Festival "Austin Film Festival")'s Distinguished Screenwriter Award. In his acceptance speech, he said that his favorites of his films were *The Wind and the Lion*, *Big Wednesday*, and *Conan*.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Milius has been married three times.[[104]](#cite_note-104) He has two children with his first wife,[[105]](#cite_note-105) and one child[[106]](#cite_note-106) with his second wife, [Celia Kaye](/wiki/Celia_Kaye "Celia Kaye").[[107]](#cite_note-107) His current marriage (since 1992) is to Elan Oberon.

Milius was a passionate surfer for much of his life but gave it up when he turned fifty.[[72]](#cite_note-crave-72)

### Views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Milius&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Views")]

Milius is a self-proclaimed "[Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen") [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism")", but he also publicly aligns himself with [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") factions in Hollywood and he was interviewed in the [documentary](/wiki/Documentary "Documentary") *[Rated R: Republicans in Hollywood](/wiki/Rated_R:_Republicans_in_Hollywood "Rated R: Republicans in Hollywood")*. He has also been consultant to a military think tank, the [Institute for Creative Technologies](/wiki/Institute_for_Creative_Technologies "Institute for Creative Technologies").[[97]](#cite_note-guardian-97) Milius said:

> I'm not a reactionary—I'm just a right-wing extremist so far beyond the [Christian Identity](/wiki/Christian_Identity "Christian Identity") people like that and stuff, that they can't even imagine. I'm so far beyond that I'm a [Maoist](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism"). I'm an anarchist. I've always been an anarchist. Any true, real right-winger if he goes far enough hates all form of government, because government should be done to cattle and not human beings.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

Milius has endorsed [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage") laws and [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription").[[109]](#cite_note-Walker-109) Milius was also quoted as saying that "it might not have been bad for this country" if [Douglas MacArthur](/wiki/Douglas_MacArthur "Douglas MacArthur") had "crossed the Mississippi like Caesar crossed the Rubicon and proclaimed himself Emperor Douglas the First."[[109]](#cite_note-Walker-109) For years, Milius was a member of the board of directors of the [National Rifle Association of America](/wiki/National_Rifle_Association_of_America "National Rifle Association of America") (NRA),[[82]](#cite_note-Maverick-82) where he was a leader (with [Charlton Heston](/wiki/Charlton_Heston "Charlton Heston")) in resisting a takeover attempt by advocates of the so-called [Militia Movement](/wiki/American_militia_movement "American militia movement").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

"I'd like to be [Jack Hawkins](/wiki/Jack_Hawkins "Jack Hawkins") in *[Bridge on the River Kwai](/wiki/The_Bridge_on_the_River_Kwai "The Bridge on the River Kwai")*", said Milius. "I call myself romantic. I believe in a lot of 19th-century ideals: chivalry, honor, loyalty, romantic love."[[111]](#cite_note-111)

In 2009, Milius signed a [petition in support of Roman Polanski](/wiki/2009_Roman_Polanski_Petition "2009 Roman Polanski Petition"), who had been detained while traveling to a film festival in relation to his 1977 [sexual abuse charges](/wiki/Roman_Polanski_sexual_abuse_case "Roman Polanski sexual abuse case"), which the petition argued would undermine the tradition of film festivals as a place for works to be shown "freely and safely", and that arresting filmmakers traveling to neutral countries could open the door "for actions of which no-one can know the effects."[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)

"On July 19, 1984, the producers of Red Dawn had a problem. In less than a month, they were due to release the director John Milius's pro-gun, survivalist action film depicting a Communist invasion of the United States, and the theatrical trailer and movie posters – both of which featured Soviet troops in or near a McDonald's restaurant – were already completed. But those materials now had to be changed because the previous day, a well-armed paranoid survivalist named James Oliver Huberty had entered a McDonald's in San Ysidro, Calif., and killed 21 people (including five children) with an Uzi submachine gun. The movie's marketing team recalled some of the posters and removed the McDonald's scene from the trailer. (The opening scene, in which the invaders gun down kids in a school, was left intact.) Red Dawn went on to become a cult classic and helped lead a generation of young men – yours truly included – into the military."
